title: Gray-Zone Maritime Rights-Protection Strategy
subtitle: Asymmetric Costs and Sustainable Presence, A Case Study of the China–Philippines Dispute over Scarborough Shoal
title_normalized: gray-zone maritime rights-protection strategy: asymmetric costs and sustainable presence, a case study of the china–philippines dispute over scarborough shoal

description: This working paper develops a Cost–Distance–Frequency (CDF) framework for evaluating gray-zone maritime rights-protection operations under asymmetric geography. Using the China–Philippines dispute over Scarborough Shoal as a case study, it models cost per effective hour of presence, risk expectation, sustainability thresholds, and manned–unmanned substitution conditions to assess how sustained presence, operational tempo, and cost compression shape long-term strategic symmetry.
abstract: Gray-zone maritime rights-protection operations increasingly hinge on sustained presence rather than episodic tactical encounters, particularly under conditions of geographic asymmetry between near-shore and far-shore actors. This paper introduces a Cost–Distance–Frequency (CDF) analytical framework that models cost per effective hour of presence, integrates risk expectation and policy-defined sustainability thresholds, and derives closed-form conditions for manned–unmanned substitution in long-duration deployments. Applying the framework to the China–Philippines dispute over Scarborough Shoal, the analysis demonstrates how near-shore advantages rooted in proximity and sortie frequency can be structurally offset over time through far-shore cost compression, prepositioning, and unmanned force integration. The study highlights the dynamic interaction among cost accumulation, operational tempo, and incident risk in shaping strategic symmetry under gray-zone conditions. By linking operational design to long-term fiscal and institutional endurance, the CDF framework offers a potentially generalizable analytical tool for informing force-posture decisions in contested maritime environments.

methodology.core_metric: Cost per effective hour of presence

main_formulas.name: Cost per effective hour of presence
Critical unmanned substitution ratio
main_formulas.formula: u* = w_m × C_u,fixed / ((A + B) × (w_u − w_m) − w_m × (C_u,var × T_on − B))

dimensions.name: Distance
dimensions.description: Transit distance and associated time, fuel, maintenance, and responsiveness penalties.

dimensions.name: Frequency
dimensions.description: Sortie frequency and turnaround rhythm determining sustained effective presence.

dimensions.name: Effective Presence
dimensions.description: Weighted on-station presence generated by manned and unmanned platforms.

dimensions.name: Risk Expectation
dimensions.description: Expected cost of loss, incident escalation, reputational exposure, and operational risk.

dimensions.name: Sustainability Threshold
dimensions.description: Policy-defined tolerance parameter reflecting fiscal, political, and institutional endurance limits.

dimensions.name: Unmanned Substitution Share
dimensions.description: Share of on-station time or presence assigned to unmanned systems under mixed deployment.

key_findings: Gray-zone maritime rights-protection contests are better understood as endurance competitions centered on sustained presence rather than episodic tactical encounters.
Near-shore advantages arise from shorter transit time, lower sortie costs, faster turnaround, and high-frequency presence, but these advantages are structurally contingent rather than permanent.
Far-shore disadvantages can be offset through cost compression, prepositioned logistics, forward nodes, platform diversification, and manned–unmanned force integration.
Cost per effective hour of presence provides a unified metric for comparing manned-only and manned–unmanned mixed deployments under long-duration gray-zone conditions.
Risk expectation and policy-defined sustainability thresholds are essential for evaluating whether a presence strategy is fiscally, institutionally, and reputationally durable.
The Scarborough Shoal case illustrates a dynamic competition between near-shore low-cost high-frequency presence and far-shore higher-cost but higher-control posturing.

figures.title: Symmetry evolution under the CDF framework (conceptual illustration)
figures.description: Conceptual diagram illustrating the qualitative evolution of strategic symmetry over time as relative cost, frequency, effective presence, and risk parameters change under the CDF framework. The figure is schematic rather than empirically calibrated.
figures.source: Author’s analysis based on the CDF framework
figures.note: Conceptual illustration; not a quantitative simulation.

disclaimer: This metadata record is generated from the uploaded PDF and updated with the earliest ORCID-derived metadata supplied by the user. The working paper provides analytical views based on public sources and does not prescribe policy choices; it examines strategic feasibility under cost–sustainability constraints.
